title: Sicilian ricotta cheesecake
subtitle: Baked Italian cheesecake with orange and candied peel
excerpt: Sicilian ricotta cheesecake is a baked Italian cheesecake made from drained ricotta, eggs, and a little sugar, scented with orange zest and vanilla. The texture is dense and slightly grainy compared to a cream cheese version, and the flavour is delicate rather than rich. A pastry shell is traditional but the filling alone, baked in a springform tin, is the simpler approach.
sourceNotes: Ricotta-based cheesecakes are a Sicilian tradition, particularly associated with Easter (the cassata siciliana uses ricotta as well). The baked ricotta cake, simpler than cassata, appears widely in southern Italian domestic cookery as a way to use surplus fresh ricotta from sheep herding. Orange zest, candied peel, and vanilla are the standard flavourings. The pastry shell derives from a sweet shortcrust (pasta frolla).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead: the texture is firmer and the orange flavour more pronounced after a night in the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 40 to 50 minutes until the edges are set and the centre has only a slight wobble when shaken

content.text: The ricotta must be well drained before you use it or the filling will be wet. Tip it into a sieve lined with muslin or a clean tea towel set over a bowl, and leave it for at least an hour in the fridge. If you are buying it the day before, drain it overnight. The finished texture should be smooth but slightly granular, closer to a set cream than a cream cheese filling.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Lightly butter a 23 cm springform tin and line the base with baking paper. In a large bowl, beat 750 g drained ricotta with 150 g caster sugar until smooth. Beat in 3 large eggs one at a time. Add the zest of 1 orange and 1 tsp vanilla extract and stir to combine. Fold in 50 g finely chopped candied peel if using. Pour into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Bake for 40 to 50 minutes until the edges are set and the centre trembles only very slightly when you shake the tin gently. Turn off the oven and leave the door ajar; leave the cake inside for 20 minutes. This prevents cracking.

Remove from the oven and cool completely in the tin on a rack. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours before unmoulding. Run a palette knife around the edge, then release the springform. Dust with icing sugar before serving. Serve at room temperature or just barely chilled.

items.problem: The cheesecake cracked across the top
items.solution: It was overbaked or cooled too quickly. The centre should still wobble when it comes from the oven. Leaving it in the cooling oven with the door ajar prevents the dramatic temperature change that causes cracking.

items.problem: The texture is grainy and wet
items.solution: The ricotta was not drained sufficiently. Drain for a full 24 hours rather than 1 to 2 hours, and squeeze lightly in the muslin before using.
